perturbation.original_text: HPS shall pay such amount to SHPS within fifteen days following the end of the applicable month.
perturbation.changed_text: HPS shall pay such amount to SHPS within thirty days following the end of the applicable month.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment deadline to 30 days is inconsistent with Florida Statute 215.422(3)(a), which generally requires payments to vendors to be made within 20 days.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute 215.422 regarding payment of vendor invoices
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 215.422(3)(a) requires state agencies to make payments to vendors within 20 days after receipt of a valid invoice. While this contract is not explicitly with a state agency, setting a 30-day payment term is generally against the spirit of prompt payment encouraged by the law. A 15-day payment term is more aligned and promotes a better understanding of prompt payments to vendors.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XIV > Chapter 215 > Section 422 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XIV TAXATION AND FINANCE Chapter 215 FINANCIAL MATTERS: GENERAL PROVISIONS Entire Chapter SECTION 422 Payments, warrants, and invoices; processing time limits; dispute resolution; agency or judicial branch compliance. F.S. 215.422 215.422 Payments, warrants, and invoices; processing time limits; dispute resolution; agency or judicial branch compliance. — (1) An invoice submitted to an agency of the state or the judicial branch, required by law to be filed with the Chief Financial Officer, shall be recorded in the financial systems of the state, approved for payment by the agency or the judicial branch, and filed with the Chief Financial Officer not later than 20 days after receipt of the invoice and receipt, inspection, and approval of the goods or services, except that in the case of a bona fide dispute the invoice recorded in the financial systems of the state shall contain a statement of the dispute and authorize payment only in the amount not disputed. The Chief Financial Officer may establish dollar thresholds and other criteria for all invoices and may delegate to a state agency or the judicial branch responsibility for maintaining the official invoices and documents for invoices which do not exceed the thresholds or which meet the established criteria. Such records shall be maintained in accordance with the requirements established by the Secretary of State. The transmission of an approved invoice recorded in the financial systems of the state to the Chief Financial Officer shall constitute filing of a request for payment of invoices for which the Chief Financial Officer has delegated to an agency custody of official records. Approval and inspection of goods or services shall take no longer than 5 working days unless the bid specifications, purchase order, or contract specifies otherwise. If an invoice filed within the 20-day period is returned by the Department of Financial Services because of an error, it shall nevertheless be deemed timely filed. The 20-day filing requirement may be waived in whole or in part by the Department of Financial Services on a showing of exceptional circumstances in accordance with rules and regulations of the department. For the purposes of determining t
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the payment deadline from 15 days to 30 days, which contradicts Florida Statute 215.422(3)(a) as explained in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that an invoice submitted to an agency of the state should be recorded and approved for payment 'not later than 20 days after receipt of the invoice and receipt, inspection, and approval of the goods or services'. Therefore, extending the payment term to 30 days violates the 20-day requirement outlined in the statute, creating a legal inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: Unless either party gives the other at least ninety days' prior written notice that it has elected not to extend the term of this Agreement beyond December 31, 1998, the term of this Agreement will be automatically extended until December 31, 1999.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless either party gives the other at least thirty days' prior written notice that it has elected not to extend the term of this Agreement beyond December 31, 1998, the term of this Agreement will be automatically extended until December 31, 1999.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for non-renewal to 30 days may violate Florida law if the contract is interpreted as a franchise agreement (even indirectly) and if 90 days’ notice is typically required to comply with good faith termination requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although not directly a franchise agreement, reducing the notification period to 30 days could create issues if the relationship is construed to have franchise-like elements. Florida franchise laws often imply requirements for good faith termination, and less notice could be viewed as a violation, particularly where reliance and significant investments are involved. A short notification period of 30 days might not provide sufficient time for a business heavily reliant on the agreement to adjust operations, secure alternative arrangements, or mitigate damages associated with the termination. A 90-day notice or more supports a more balanced approach.

F.S. 817.416 817.416 Franchises and distributorships; misrepresentations. — (1) DEFINITIONS. — For the purpose of this section: (a) The term “person” means an individual, partnership, corporation, association, or other entity doing business in Florida. (b) The term “franchise or distributorship” means a contract or agreement, either expressed or implied, whether oral or written, between two or more persons: 1. Wherein a commercial relationship of definite duration or continuing indefinite duration is involved; 2. Wherein one party, hereinafter called the “franchisee,” is granted the right to offer, sell, and distribute goods or services manufactured, processed, distributed or, in the case of services, organized and directed by another party; 3. Wherein the franchisee as an independent business constitutes a component of franchisor’s distribution system; and 4. Wherein the operation of the franchisee’s business franchise is substantially reliant on franchisors for the basic supply of goods. (c) The term “goods” means any article or thing without limitation, or any part of such article or thing, including any article or thing used or consumed by a franchisee in rendering a service established, organized, directed, or approved by a franchisor. (2) DECLARATIONS. — (a) It is unlawful, when selling or establishing a franchise or distributorship, for any person: 1. Intentionally to misrepresent the prospects or chances for success of a proposed or existing franchise or distributorship; 2. Intentionally to misrepresent, by failure to disclose or otherwise, the known required total investment for such franchise or distributorship; or 3. Intentionally to misrepresent or fail to disclose efforts to sell or establish more franchises or distributorships than is reasonable to expect the market or market area for the particular franchise or distributorship to sustain. (b) The execution or carrying out of a scheme, plan, or corporate organization which violates any of the provisions of this se
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period from 90 days to 30 days. The Perturbation and Law Explanations suggest this could be problematic if the agreement is construed as a franchise or has franchise-like elements, potentially violating good faith termination requirements under Florida law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly section 817.416, defines franchises and distributorships and addresses misrepresentations related to them, it does not explicitly state a 90-day notice requirement. However, the explanations highlight that reducing the notice period might not provide sufficient time for a business reliant on the agreement to adjust operations, which could be seen as a violation of good faith, implying a contradiction of legal standards related to fair dealing in franchise-like relationships.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that either party materially or repeatedly defaults in the performance of any of its duties or obligations hereunder and does not substantially cure such default within thirty days after being given written notice specifying the default, or, with respect to those defaults which cannot reasonably be cured within thirty days, if the defaulting party fails to proceed promptly after being given such notice to commence curing the default and thereafter to reasonably proceed to cure the same, then the party not in default 2 may, by giving written notice to the defaulting party, terminate this Agreement as of a date specified in such notice of termination.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that either party materially or repeatedly defaults in the performance of any of its duties or obligations hereunder and does not substantially cure such default within ten days after being given written notice specifying the default, or, with respect to those defaults which cannot reasonably be cured within ten days, if the defaulting party fails to proceed promptly after being given such notice to commence curing the default and thereafter to reasonably proceed to cure the same, then the party not in default 2 may, by giving written notice to the defaulting party, terminate this Agreement as of a date specified in such notice of termination.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period to ten days for a material default might be deemed commercially unreasonable and conflict with general principles of contract law regarding adequate opportunity to cure. This could be considered a breach of good faith, even though Florida doesn't have a specific statute detailing cure periods for all commercial contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While Florida doesn't specify a statutory minimum cure period, reducing it to 10 days for 'material' defaults may violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing inherent in all contracts. A 10-day cure period might not be sufficient time to rectify a significant breach, particularly if it involves complex issues or requires substantial resources. A 30-day cure period, or a period tailored to the specific nature of the default, is more likely to satisfy the implied duty to provide a reasonable opportunity for the breaching party to fulfill its obligations, thereby upholding the contract's intended purpose and avoiding unfair termination.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cure period for a material default from 30 days to 10 days. The Law Explanation indicates this reduction could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which requires a reasonable opportunity to fulfill obligations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines good faith as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'observance of fair dealing standards.' Reducing the cure period significantly could be viewed as failing to observe fair dealing standards, thus creating a legal contradiction by potentially breaching the implied covenant of good faith.
